# Literature Review

**Project:** Sparse Rationale-Constrained Attention for Hate Speech Detection
**Date:** 2026-04-01
**Generated by:** /research-init (Step 3)
**Papers reviewed:** 58 (research-landscape) + 5 (cross-field)
**Tier-1 papers:** 18

---

## Executive Summary

The core technical innovation — applying sparsemax as the attention activation in gradient-importance-selected BERT heads, supervised with token-level rationale masks, on HateXplain — has no direct prior art. The closest concurrent work is SRA (Eilertsen et al., AAAI 2025), which uses a single fixed head (layer 8, head 7) with softmax and MSE loss. Our contribution extends SRA along three independent axes: (A1) principled head selection via gradient importance, (A2) sparsemax as the supervised head's activation, (A3) IG-based faithfulness evaluation with 5-seed bootstrap CIs.

**Novelty verdict:** CLEAR (Pass N1 gate). Cite-and-differentiate required for Ross et al. 2017 and concurrent work SRA/SMRA.

---

## Cluster 1: Rationale-Supervised Attention (Core Cluster)

### 1.1 Direct Concurrent Work

**Eilertsen et al. (2025) — Supervised Rational Attention (SRA)**
- arXiv:2511.07065 | AAAI 2025
- *Method*: MSE alignment loss between CLS attention at layer 8, head 7 and majority-vote binary rationale mask from HateXplain. Applied only to hate/offensive examples with non-empty masks.
- *Results*: IoU-F1 = 0.539 (4.5× over vanilla BERT baseline), F1 = 0.671.
- *Limitations*: Head fixed without importance analysis; softmax attention (mismatch with sparse binary target); ≤3 seeds, no bootstrap CIs; LIME used for faithfulness (high variance on ~20-token posts).
- **Tier: 1** | **Threat: MEDIUM** | **Relation: Cite-and-differentiate**

**Vargas et al. (2026) — Supervised Moral Rationale Attention (SMRA)**
- arXiv:2601.03481 | 2026
- *Method*: Extends SRA with Moral Foundations Theory-based rationale supervision (6 moral dimensions). Portuguese-centric evaluation. Same softmax-only architecture as SRA.
- *Results*: +7.4 pp IoU-F1 on Portuguese dataset; same HateXplain baseline as SRA.
- *Limitations*: Same mechanistic limitations as SRA; no cross-lingual generalization.
- **Tier: 1** | **Threat: LOW** | **Relation: Cite as concurrent; English contribution differs**

### 1.2 Rationale Extraction and Evaluation

**Mathew et al. (2021) — HateXplain**
- AAAI 2021 | DOI: 10.1609/aaai.v35i17.17745
- *Method*: 20,148 English posts from Twitter/Gab, 3-class (hate/offensive/normal), 3 annotators, token-level rationale spans.
- *Key statistics*: Median token coverage ~30% (sparse rationale targets). ~31% test examples have annotation inconsistencies.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Primary benchmark**

**DeYoung et al. (2020) — ERASER**
- ACL 2020
- *Method*: Standardized evaluation framework: comprehensiveness = performance drop when predicted rationale tokens are removed; sufficiency = performance with only rationale tokens.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Defines our faithfulness evaluation protocol**

**Wiegreffe & Pinter (2019) — Attention is Not Not Explanation**
- EMNLP 2019
- *Method*: Tests whether attention distributions can be adversarially manipulated; concludes attention can be used as explanation when controlled.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Methodological context for supervised attention**

**Jain & Wallace (2019) — Attention is Not Explanation**
- NAACL 2019
- *Method*: Demonstrates attention weights frequently uncorrelated with gradient-based feature importance; different attention distributions can produce identical predictions.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Motivates supervised attention (passive attention is unreliable)**

### 1.3 Multi-Relational Probing (MRP)

- Ma et al. (EMNLP 2023): Multi-head attention probing with relational supervision. Uses all heads simultaneously with soft label supervision. No sparsemax; no importance selection.
- **Tier: 2** | **Relation: Cite as related supervised-attention work**

---

## Cluster 2: Sparse Attention Mechanisms

**Martins & Astudillo (2016) — Sparsemax**
- ICML 2016
- *Method*: Projects onto the probability simplex via Euclidean projection; produces exact-zero weights for non-attended tokens. Sparsemax loss (convex conjugate) penalizes support mismatch.
- *Results*: Comparable NMT performance to softmax; 90% of attention weights exactly zero.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Core technical component**

**Correia et al. (2019) — Adaptively Sparse Transformers**
- EMNLP 2019
- *Method*: α-entmax with per-head learnable α; heads naturally learn different sparsity preferences. Applied to NMT.
- *Results*: Improved head diversity and interpretability vs. softmax. Heads with α→2 behave like sparsemax; with α→1 like softmax.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Technical background; future extension to per-head α**

**Peters & Martins (2019) — Sparse Sequence-to-Sequence Models**
- arXiv:1905.05702
- *Method*: Extends sparsemax to seq2seq generation tasks; shows sparse attention improves interpretability without classification performance loss.
- **Tier: 2** | **Relation: Technical background**

---

## Cluster 3: BERT Head Specialization and Selection

**Clark et al. (2019) — What Does BERT Look At?**
- BlackboxNLP 2019
- *Method*: Probes each BERT head; identifies specialization: syntactic heads (dependency parsing), positional heads (attend to [SEP]/preceding token), semantic heads.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Motivates head selection (not all heads are equal)**

**Voita et al. (2019) — Analyzing Multi-Head Self-Attention**
- ACL 2019
- *Method*: Identifies "important" heads by LRP scores; shows ~80% of heads can be pruned without performance loss. Pruned heads show positional/syntactic patterns; remaining heads show semantic specialization.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Gradient importance formula implementation**

**Michel et al. (2019) — Are Sixteen Heads Really Better than One?**
- NeurIPS 2019
- *Method*: Gradient-based head importance scoring I(h,l) = E_x[|∂L_CE/∂A^{CLS,h,l}|]. Iterative pruning from least to most important.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Provides exact head selection formula for our method**

---

## Cluster 4: Hate Speech Detection

**Davidson et al. (2017) — Automated Hate Speech Detection**
- ICWSM 2017
- *Method*: Hate/offensive/neither classification; n-gram features + logistic regression. Establishes hate vs. offensive distinction.
- **Tier: 2**

**Founta et al. (2018) — Large Scale Crowdsourcing and Characterization**
- ICWSM 2018
- *Method*: 80K tweets, 4-class labeling via crowdsourcing. Hate/abusive/spam/normal.
- **Tier: 2**

**Vidgen et al. (2021) — Learning from the Worst**
- ACL 2021
- *Method*: Adversarial data augmentation for hate speech; dynamic data collection with human-in-the-loop to create challenging examples.
- **Tier: 2** | **Relation: Generalization challenge beyond HateXplain**

**Zampieri et al. (2019) — OLID / OffComEval**
- SemEval 2019
- *Method*: Offensive Language Identification Dataset. 14K tweets, hierarchical offense taxonomy.
- **Tier: 3**

---

## Cluster 5: Annotator Agreement and Disagreement

**Sap et al. (2022) — Annotators with Attitudes**
- *Method*: Shows annotator identity (race, gender) predicts disagreement on implicit bias detection.
- **Tier: 2** | **Relation: Motivates annotator agreement stratification (H6)**

**Davani et al. (2022) — Dealing with Disagreements**
- *Method*: Disagreement-aware models; represents annotator disagreement explicitly in training.
- **Tier: 2**

**D3CODE (2024) — Cross-Cultural Disagreement**
- EMNLP 2024
- *Method*: Annotator moral values explain offensiveness disagreement more strongly than demographic factors. Cross-cultural agreement higher for explicit hate (slurs) than subtle discrimination.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Directly motivates H6 (annotator agreement stratification experiment)**

---

## Cluster 6: Faithfulness Evaluation and Attribution Methods

**Sundararajan et al. (2017) — Integrated Gradients**
- ICML 2017
- *Method*: Satisfies Sensitivity (relevant features get non-zero attribution) and Implementation Invariance axioms. Path-integral from baseline to input.
- **Tier: 1** | **Relation: Theoretically correct replacement for LIME in faithfulness evaluation (H4)**

**Ribeiro et al. (2016) — LIME**
- KDD 2016
- *Method*: Local surrogate approximation via random perturbation; high variance on short texts (~20 tokens).
- **Tier: 2** | **Relation: Problematic prior evaluator; validated by H4**

**Samek et al. (2017) — Evaluating Visualization of Neural Network Predictions**
- *Method*: AOPC metric for evaluating feature importance; precursor to comprehensiveness.
- **Tier: 3**

---

## Cross-Field Related Work

### Explanation-Guided Learning (CV)

**Ross et al. (2017) — Right for the Right Reasons**
- IJCAI 2017 | arXiv:1703.03717
- *Method*: Constrains input gradients (∂L/∂x) to align with human binary annotation masks via MSE penalty.
- *Differential*: Operates in gradient space (not attention space); L2 penalty (not sparsemax activation); no multi-head selection; image/tabular binary classification (not text multi-class).
- **Relation: Cite-and-differentiate; cross-field EGL precursor**

**Shu et al. (2025) — Hybrid Explanation-Guided Learning**
- MICCAI 2025 | arXiv:2510.12704
- *Method*: Combines self-supervised and human-guided attention constraints for ViT in chest X-ray.
- *Differential*: ViT patch attention (not BERT CLS token attention); no head selection; no sparsemax; medical imaging (not text).
- **Relation: Cite as parallel motivation for EGL in transformer attention**

**Chriqui et al. (2025) — HuMAL**
- arXiv:2502.06811
- *Method*: Human-Machine Attention Learning; aligns BERT/GPT-2/XLNet attention with self-reported human attention for sentiment/personality.
- *Differential*: No head selection or importance scoring; no sparsemax; self-reported (not expert crowd-annotated) rationales; sentiment/personality (not hate speech).
- **Relation: Cite as parallel independent motivation**

**Koh et al. (2020) — Concept Bottleneck Models**
- ICML 2020 | arXiv:2007.04612
- *Method*: Predicts human-specified concept activations at intermediate bottleneck before final prediction.
- *Differential*: Replaces intermediate activation with concept prediction (not modify attention activation function); no attention weights; no head selection.
- **Relation: Cite for general intermediate supervision principle**

---

## Key Gaps Confirmed by Literature Review

| Gap ID | Description | Supporting Evidence |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| G1 | No paper applies sparsemax as attention activation under rationale supervision | SRA (softmax only), no sparsemax in supervised-attention literature |
| G2 | No paper uses gradient importance to select which heads to supervise | SRA: fixed head 7/8 with no justification; all MRP work supervises all heads |
| G3 | SRA/SMRA use ≤3 seeds; no bootstrap CIs reported | Statistical power ~30% at n=3; NeurIPS standards require credible CIs |
| G4 | LIME stability on short social media texts (~20 tokens) never validated | LIME instability well-known; IG axiomatically superior (Sundararajan et al.) |
| G5 | Rationale supervision effectiveness never stratified by annotator agreement | D3CODE shows agreement reflects genuine moral disagreement, not noise |
| G6 | Loss function comparison (MSE vs KL vs sparsemax_loss) never done in supervised attention | SRA uses MSE with no justification; KL and sparsemax_loss are natural candidates |

---

## Reading Priority

**Read immediately (blocks experiments):**
1. SRA full text — arXiv:2511.07065 (implementation details, exact hyperparameters)
2. HateXplain — AAAI 2021 (dataset statistics, annotation protocol)
3. Sparsemax — ICML 2016 (sparsemax loss implementation)
4. Michel et al. 2019 — NeurIPS 2019 (head importance formula)
5. ERASER — ACL 2020 (comprehensiveness/sufficiency implementation)

**Read before writing (related work section):**
6. Clark et al. 2019, Voita et al. 2019 (head specialization background)
7. α-entmax — EMNLP 2019 (technical context)
8. Ross et al. 2017 (cross-field EGL; differential must be precise)
9. Jain & Wallace 2019, Wiegreffe & Pinter 2019 (attention debate context)
10. D3CODE 2024 (annotator agreement motivation)
